behaviors.description: The clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is extraction quality, not module wiring — a single reviewer step. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network dependency required. LLM-DEP: 'Identifies all key terms' is a quality claim about AI output. Whether the extractor correctly finds indirect party references or implicit obligations depends entirely on what a real LLM produces — mocking the LLM would reduce this to a prompt-formatting test, hiding real extraction failures. Running 100 times with a real model could yield different recall rates. MULTI-STEP: Single reviewer, no sequence. REJECTION-GATE: Integration rejected — the failure is not at a module handoff boundary but in the AI's extraction capability itself.
behaviors.test_description: Send a realistic legal contract containing parties (named and referenced pronominally), dates (explicit and relative), obligations (direct and implied), and penalties (stated and conditional) to the real clause extractor. Assert that all expected terms appear in the output with correct categorization. Run with the real LLM backend; do not mock the model call.

behaviors.description: The risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: The phrase 'even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic' is an explicit quality claim requiring semantic understanding of nuanced language — exactly the kind of capability that varies across real model runs and cannot be verified by mocking. A mocked model would return a fixed answer, making the test vacuous. MOCK-MISS: The failure mode lives in the model's semantic judgment, not in module wiring or data plumbing. REAL-INFRA: No OS/subprocess dependency. REJECTION-GATE: Integration rejected — wiring between the risk assessor module and the rest of the pipeline is irrelevant to whether indirect phrasing is caught; that is a pure AI capability question.
behaviors.test_description: Construct test contracts containing risk-policy-violating clauses expressed via euphemism or indirection (e.g., 'the party may seek appropriate remedies' instead of 'penalty clause'). Submit each to the real risk assessor with the configured risk policy loaded. Assert that each problematic clause is flagged with a risk annotation. Use the real LLM; do not mock model output.

behaviors.description: The compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: 'Correctly determines' compliance is a quality claim: the compliance checker must reason over jurisdiction-specific regulatory knowledge. Whether a clause satisfies a regulatory requirement is a semantic judgment that varies with real model behavior. Mocking would substitute a fixed answer for real reasoning, hiding the genuine failure mode. MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the model's compliance reasoning, not in module-to-module data handoff. REAL-INFRA: No OS/subprocess dependency. REJECTION-GATE: System rejected — no real network or subprocess is inherently required; the real dependency is the LLM's regulatory reasoning capability.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the compliance checker for a specific jurisdiction (e.g., GDPR for EU contracts). Provide one contract that satisfies all applicable requirements and one that violates at least one requirement. Submit both to the real compliance checker. Assert that the compliant contract receives a passing determination and the non-compliant contract is flagged with the specific violated requirement. Use the real LLM backend.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments (e.g., extractor says 'standard' but risk assessor says 'high risk'), the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is in the merge algorithm's handling of conflicting inputs — a deterministic function that takes structured reviewer outputs and produces a unified report. The failure does not require multiple modules to interact; it lives entirely within the merge function's logic. REAL-INFRA: No OS or network dependency. LLM-DEP: The merge step is deterministic given its inputs; correctness does not depend on what a real LLM produces. Reviewer outputs can be provided as fixed test data. MOCK-HIDE: Providing pre-constructed conflicting reviewer outputs does not hide the failure — the merge algorithm must still handle the conflict correctly. REJECTION-GATE: Integration rejected — I considered whether the merge step requires multiple modules to wire together, but the described behavior is a single algorithmic boundary (the merge function); providing mock reviewer-output structs is sufficient to expose a real failure.
behaviors.test_description: Construct two pre-built reviewer result objects for the same clause ID: one with assessment='standard' attributed to the clause extractor, and one with assessment='high risk' attributed to the risk assessor. Pass both directly to the merge function. Assert that the returned unified report entry contains both assessments and that each is attributed to the correct reviewer. No real LLM calls needed.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report — completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: This behavior explicitly describes multiple agentic steps in sequence: dispatch to three parallel AI reviewers (each an independent LLM call), collect all results, then merge. This is a multi-step agentic pipeline by definition. LLM-DEP: 'For a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings' requires real AI calls to produce real findings; mocking any reviewer would trivialize the collection and merge verification. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking reviewers would hide failures in parallel dispatch (e.g., one reviewer's result silently dropped), in async result collection, or in merge completeness — the exact failure modes the behavior targets. REAL-INFRA: Parallel dispatch likely involves async I/O or concurrency primitives that are part of the real infrastructure. REJECTION-GATE: Agentic rejected — a single agentic call cannot expose failures in parallel dispatch coordination or result collection across three independent reviewers; the failure boundary spans multiple agentic steps in sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a real legal contract to the full pipeline with all three reviewers using real LLM backends. After the pipeline completes, assert: (a) the unified report contains at least one finding attributed to each of the three reviewers, (b) no reviewer's output is silently absent or overwritten, and (c) the report is structurally valid. Run the pipeline end-to-end without mocking any reviewer or the merge step.
